questions.question: What is the constitutional basis for the right to silence in Irish law?

options.A: The right to silence is purely a common law privilege — it has no constitutional basis in Irish law
options.B: The right to silence in Irish law is constitutionally grounded under Article 38.1 (right to a fair trial) and Article 40.3 (guarantee of personal rights including the right against self-incrimination); the courts have recognised that the right not to incriminate oneself is an implied constitutional right protected under these provisions
options.C: The right to silence is established exclusively by the European Convention on Human Rights — it has no independent domestic constitutional basis
options.D: The right to silence is established by s.18 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 — the statute creates the right

questions.explanation: The right to silence in Irish law has constitutional foundations. It is grounded in: (1) Article 38.1 — which provides that no person shall be tried on any criminal charge save in due course of law; the right to a fair trial implicit in Art 38.1 encompasses the right not to be compelled to provide evidence against oneself; and (2) Article 40.3 — which guarantees personal rights, from which the courts have derived the right against self-incrimination. The right to silence is also protected by Article 6 of the European Convention on Human Rights (right to a fair trial) which Ireland must comply with under the ECHR Act 2003. The right exists at common law as well. Options A and C incorrectly deny or limit the constitutional basis. Option D incorrectly identifies ss.18-19 CJA 1984 as the source of the right — those sections actually create limitations on (adverse inferences from) the right, not the right itself.

questions.question: Sections 18 and 19 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 permit adverse inferences to be drawn from an accused's failure to mention certain matters. What do these provisions specifically provide?

options.A: Sections 18 and 19 abolish the right to silence entirely — an accused must answer all questions put by the Gardaí or face automatic conviction
options.B: Section 18 provides that a court or jury may draw adverse inferences where an accused, during detention, fails to mention a fact when questioned that they later rely on in their defence — if the accused could reasonably have been expected to mention the fact at the time and they did not, an inference can be drawn; s.19 provides a similar adverse inference provision for failure to account for objects, marks, or substances
options.C: Sections 18 and 19 provide that a suspect's silence during Garda questioning can alone be the basis for conviction — no other evidence is required
options.D: Sections 18 and 19 apply only to murder investigations — they have no application to other offences

questions.explanation: Sections 18 and 19 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 create specific adverse inference provisions that limit (but do not abolish) the right to silence in certain circumstances. Section 18: where an accused, during detention at a Garda station, fails to mention a fact when being questioned that they later rely on as part of their defence at trial, and it was reasonably possible to mention that fact at the time, the court or jury may draw such inferences as appear proper. Section 19: a similar adverse inference provision applies where an accused fails to account for any object, substance, or mark when asked to do so by a Garda during detention. These provisions create a modified right to silence rather than abolishing it — silence remains available but may carry adverse consequences in certain circumstances. Options A and C incorrectly abolish the right. Option D incorrectly limits the provisions to murder investigations.

questions.question: Section 2(1) of the Criminal Justice Act 1995 creates a specific adverse inference provision. What does it provide, and what is its key procedural requirement?

options.A: Section 2(1) CJA 1995 abolishes the right to silence for all indictable offences — silence in all circumstances is treated as evidence of guilt
options.B: Section 2(1) CJA 1995 provides that where a suspect, having been cautioned in the special form required by the section, fails to mention a fact when questioned that they later rely on in their defence, an adverse inference may be drawn; the key procedural requirement is that the special caution must be administered — the standard caution ('you are not obliged to say anything...') is insufficient; the Garda must specifically warn the suspect that adverse inferences may be drawn from their silence
options.C: Section 2(1) CJA 1995 applies only to revenue and tax offences — it has no application in ordinary criminal proceedings
options.D: Section 2(1) CJA 1995 requires the suspect to be given written notice of the adverse inference provision at least 24 hours before questioning begins

questions.explanation: Section 2(1) of the Criminal Justice Act 1995 creates an adverse inference provision with a critical procedural safeguard: the special caution requirement. The section provides that adverse inferences may be drawn from a suspect's failure to mention a relevant fact during questioning — but only if the Garda administered the special caution specifically warning the suspect that adverse inferences may be drawn from their silence. The standard Garda caution ('You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so, but anything you do say will be taken down in writing and may be given in evidence') is not sufficient — the special caution for s.2(1) must specifically include the warning about adverse inferences. This procedural safeguard ensures the suspect is aware of the potential consequences of silence before choosing to exercise the right. Failure to administer the special caution means no adverse inference can be drawn. Options A and C misstate the scope. Option D invents a 24-hour advance notice requirement.

questions.question: Murray v United Kingdom [1996] ECtHR (Grand Chamber) is a seminal case on the right to silence and adverse inferences. What did the court hold?

options.A: The court held that any adverse inference from silence automatically violates Article 6 ECHR — adverse inference regimes are incompatible with the right to a fair trial
options.B: The court held in Murray v UK [1996] ECtHR that drawing adverse inferences from silence does not of itself violate Article 6 ECHR (right to a fair trial) provided that appropriate safeguards are in place — in particular, the accused must have access to legal advice before and during questioning; the silence itself cannot be the sole or main basis for a conviction; these safeguards ensure that the right to silence retains practical meaning even within an adverse inference regime
options.C: The court held that the right to silence is absolute under the ECHR and cannot be qualified in any circumstances
options.D: Murray v UK [1996] concerned the right to a speedy trial — it has no relevance to the right to silence

questions.explanation: Murray v United Kingdom [1996] ECtHR (Grand Chamber) is the leading ECtHR authority on adverse inferences and the right to silence. The case arose from Northern Ireland (Criminal Evidence (Northern Ireland) Order 1988, which was similar to the Irish adverse inference provisions). The court held that drawing adverse inferences from silence does not automatically violate Article 6 ECHR provided adequate safeguards are in place. The key safeguards required by Murray are: (1) Access to legal advice before and during questioning — the accused must have had the opportunity to consult a solicitor; and (2) The adverse inference cannot be the sole or main basis for conviction — there must be other evidence; silence alone is insufficient for conviction. Murray is important because it confirms that adverse inference regimes (like ss.18-19 CJA 1984) are compatible with the ECHR when properly safeguarded. Options A and C are directly contrary to the holding. Option D incorrectly identifies the subject matter of Murray.

questions.question: Can an adverse inference from silence alone be sufficient to convict an accused in Irish law?

options.A: Yes — once the special caution has been administered and the accused has remained silent, their silence alone is sufficient to support conviction
options.B: No — adverse inferences from silence cannot alone found a conviction in Irish law; the adverse inference from silence operates as supporting evidence but there must be other evidence against the accused; a conviction based solely on the accused's silence would be incompatible with the right to a fair trial under Art 38.1 of the Constitution and Art 6 ECHR as confirmed in Murray v UK [1996]
options.C: Yes — but only for minor summary offences; for serious indictable offences, additional evidence is required
options.D: Whether silence alone can support conviction is unresolved in Irish law — there are conflicting authorities on this point

questions.explanation: Adverse inferences from silence cannot alone found a conviction in Irish law. This is a fundamental safeguard preserved by both domestic constitutional law and the ECHR. As confirmed in Murray v UK [1996], silence must not be the sole or main basis for conviction — there must be other evidence from which the adverse inference is drawn as an additional element. The right to silence, even in its qualified form under the adverse inference regime, retains this core protection. In practice, this means that the prosecution must establish a prima facie case against the accused from other evidence before the adverse inference from silence can operate as a supplementary element supporting the prosecution case. Reliance on silence alone for conviction would violate Art 38.1 (fair trial) and Art 6 ECHR. Option A incorrectly allows conviction on silence alone after the special caution. Option C introduces an unfounded distinction between summary and indictable offences. Option D incorrectly characterises the law as unresolved.

questions.question: Damien is arrested and detained under s.4 CJA 1984. He is cautioned and then refuses to answer any questions about a robbery the Gardaí believe he committed. He is later charged and at trial relies on an alibi that he was at a friend's house at the time. Can the court draw an adverse inference from Damien's failure to mention the alibi during questioning?

options.A: No — the right to silence is absolute and no inference can ever be drawn from silence during Garda questioning
options.B: Potentially yes — under s.18 CJA 1984, where an accused, during detention, fails to mention a fact when questioned that they later rely on in their defence, and it was reasonably possible to mention it at the time, an adverse inference may be drawn; the court can consider whether it was reasonable for Damien not to have mentioned his alibi during questioning, and if so, may draw an inference that the alibi is a recent fabrication or that his evidence lacks weight
options.C: No — adverse inferences can only be drawn from silence about the offence itself, not from failure to mention a positive defence such as an alibi
options.D: No — adverse inferences under s.18 CJA 1984 only apply where the accused has made a partial statement; total silence during questioning cannot give rise to an inference

questions.explanation: Section 18 CJA 1984 applies directly to this scenario. The provision permits adverse inferences where an accused, during detention, fails to mention a fact when questioned that they later rely on at trial in their defence. Damien's alibi — that he was at a friend's house — is a fact that he could reasonably have been expected to mention during questioning if it were true. By staying silent and not mentioning the alibi during questioning, and then raising it at trial, the court may draw an adverse inference (for example, that the alibi is a recent fabrication invented for the purposes of trial). The adverse inference does not mean Damien is guilty — it simply permits the court to treat the lateness of the alibi as a relevant consideration in assessing its credibility. The safeguards from Murray v UK must be observed (e.g., legal advice must have been available). Option A incorrectly treats the right as absolute. Option C incorrectly excludes defences from the inference regime. Option D incorrectly requires partial statements.

questions.question: What is the significance of the right to legal advice in the context of the right to silence and adverse inference provisions?

options.A: The right to legal advice is unrelated to the right to silence — they are entirely separate procedural rights
options.B: The right to legal advice is critically linked to the right to silence and the adverse inference regime: as confirmed in Murray v UK [1996] ECtHR, access to legal advice before and during questioning is a key safeguard ensuring that adverse inference regimes do not violate Art 6 ECHR; a suspect who is denied access to a solicitor and then makes no comment cannot have adverse inferences properly drawn from their silence — the denial of legal advice taints any adverse inference; the accused must be able to make an informed decision about whether to speak
options.C: The right to legal advice requires that a solicitor be physically present in the interview room throughout all questioning — advice over the phone is insufficient
options.D: Legal advice is only relevant where the accused subsequently seeks to rely on the advice as a reason for their silence — where the accused offers no reason for silence, legal advice is irrelevant

questions.explanation: The right to legal advice and the right to silence are deeply interconnected in the adverse inference context. Murray v UK [1996] ECtHR made clear that access to legal advice is one of the key safeguards ensuring that adverse inference regimes comply with Article 6 ECHR. The rationale is that a person cannot make an informed decision about whether to exercise their right to silence (and accept the potential consequences) without legal advice. If the Gardaí deny a detained person access to a solicitor before questioning, and the person then remains silent, it would be unfair to allow adverse inferences to be drawn from that silence — the person did not have the information needed to make an informed choice. In practice, the Garda Síochána Custody Regulations require detained persons to be informed of and given access to the right to legal advice before questioning. Option A incorrectly severs the connection. Option C incorrectly requires in-person attendance throughout. Option D incorrectly marginalises the role of legal advice.

questions.question: Does the right to silence apply equally during Garda questioning at the investigation stage and at trial?

options.A: The right to silence applies only at trial — there is no right to silence during Garda questioning in the investigation stage
options.B: The right to silence applies at both stages but in different ways: at the investigation stage (Garda questioning), the right is protected but subject to the adverse inference provisions of ss.18-19 CJA 1984 and s.2(1) CJA 1995; at trial, the accused has the right not to testify and silence at trial cannot give rise to adverse inferences (the accused is not a compellable witness); the accused cannot be compelled to give evidence in their own defence
options.C: The right to silence at trial has been abolished by the Criminal Justice Act 2007 — all accused persons must now testify
options.D: The right to silence is identical at both stages — the adverse inference provisions have no application at trial

questions.explanation: The right to silence applies differently at the investigation stage and at trial. At the investigation stage (Garda questioning during detention): the right to silence exists but is qualified by the adverse inference provisions — silence may have consequences if the accused later relies on facts not mentioned during questioning. At trial: the accused has the right not to give evidence (s.1 Criminal Evidence Act 1898 provides that the accused is a competent but not compellable witness); the accused cannot be forced to testify; and the jury must not draw adverse inferences from the accused's decision not to testify. The judge must direct the jury that the accused's silence at trial cannot be used against them. This is an important distinction — the adverse inference regime under ss.18-19 CJA 1984 operates during detention, not in respect of the accused's decision not to testify at trial. Option A incorrectly denies any right during investigation. Option C invents abolition of the right to silence at trial. Option D incorrectly treats both stages as identical.

questions.question: Elena is arrested and cautioned with the standard caution ('You are not obliged to say anything...'). She is then questioned about a drugs offence and remains silent. The Gardaí did not administer a special caution under the Criminal Justice Act 1995. At trial, the prosecution seeks to have an adverse inference drawn from Elena's silence under s.2(1) CJA 1995. Is this permissible?

options.A: Yes — the standard caution is sufficient to allow adverse inferences to be drawn under s.2(1) CJA 1995
options.B: No — s.2(1) CJA 1995 requires that the special caution be administered specifically warning Elena that adverse inferences may be drawn from her silence; the standard caution is not sufficient; as the special caution was not administered, the adverse inference provision in s.2(1) CJA 1995 cannot be invoked; the failure to administer the special caution means Elena cannot be penalised for exercising her right to silence during questioning
options.C: Yes — once Elena was cautioned in any form, all adverse inference provisions are automatically available to the prosecution
options.D: The issue is discretionary — the court can decide whether to draw adverse inferences regardless of which caution was administered

questions.explanation: The adverse inference under s.2(1) CJA 1995 is not permissible on these facts because the special caution was not administered. Section 2(1) requires the Garda to have administered the special caution — which specifically warns the suspect that adverse inferences may be drawn from their failure to mention relevant facts. The standard caution ('You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so...') is the routine caution that informs a person of the general right to silence. It does not include the specific warning about adverse inferences required by s.2(1). Since Elena was only given the standard caution, not the special caution, the prosecution cannot rely on s.2(1) to invite adverse inferences from her silence. This procedural requirement is a significant safeguard — it ensures the suspect is specifically warned of the potential consequences before the adverse inference regime applies. Option A incorrectly accepts the standard caution as sufficient for s.2(1). Option C invents a general adverse inference availability from any caution. Option D incorrectly characterises the issue as discretionary.
